name: Wisconsin
tooltip: Wisconsin — Cat 2: Right of privacy statute. 2025 AI intimate-image act. Common-law publicity from Hirsch v. S.C. Johnson.
rightOfPublicity: Yes — Wis. Stat. § 995.50 (titled "Right of privacy"; appropriation prong at (2)(am)2.). True commercial publicity rights are also recognized at common law under *Hirsch v. S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.*, 90 Wis. 2d 379, 280 N.W.2d 129 (1979) — one of the most cited common-law right-of-publicity cases in the country.
postMortem: No codified post-mortem term. Common-law treatment uncertain.
aiCoverage: Yes. 2023 Wis. Act 123 (Wis. Stat. § 11.1303(2m), election synthetic media, signed March 21, 2024). 2025 Wis. Act 34 (Wis. Stat. § 942.09 synthetic intimate representations). § 995.50(2)(d) expressly cross-references § 942.09 conduct as actionable invasion of privacy.
remedy: Civil suit under § 995.50 with $1,000 statutory floor plus actual damages and attorney's fees. Common-law publicity suit under *Hirsch* for true commercial appropriation cases. For AI-generated intimate images, civil action under § 995.50(2)(d) cross-referencing § 942.09.
whatsInteresting: Wisconsin is famous in right-of-publicity law for *Hirsch* — Elroy "Crazylegs" Hirsch, the football star, sued a shaving gel company for using "Crazylegs" as a product name, and the Wisconsin Supreme Court used the case to recognize a robust common-law right of publicity. That case has been cited in dozens of other states' opinions ever since. Statutorily, Wisconsin is privacy-tort only, but the common law is muscular. The 2025 AI intimate representations act creates a clean civil hook by cross-referencing the privacy statute.
primarySources: *Hirsch v. S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.*, 90 Wis. 2d 379 (1979)
